title: Hepatocellular Carcinoma
oneLiner: Primary liver malignancy arising in chronic liver disease, often cirrhosis. Leading cause of death in cirrhosis, diagnosed on surveillance or when decompensation occurs.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Variceal haemorrhage** – haematemesis, melaena with tachycardia/hypotension. Single finding: **fresh blood on nasogastric tube** (if placed) or **visible red/blue varices on oesophagogastroduodenoscopy (OGD) – but do not delay resuscitation for endoscopy.**
- **Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP)** – fever, abdominal pain, encephalopathy. Single finding: **ascitic neutrophil count >250 cells/µL** (but start antibiotics if clinical suspicion + high lactate).
- **Hepatic encephalopathy** – confusion, asterixis, drowsy. Single finding: **reversed sleep-wake cycle + asterixis** (not just sepsis).
- **Tumour rupture with haemorrhage** – sudden severe right upper quadrant pain, hypotension. Single finding: **free fluid on bedside US + hypotension** (rare but immediately lethal; needs embolisation or emergency surgery).
sections.Presentation:
- Often **asymptomatic** – discovered on surveillance US in cirrhotic patients
- **Right upper quadrant pain** (dull ache or sharp if rupture)
- **Unintentional weight loss / anorexia**
- **Worsening jaundice, ascites, or encephalopathy** (new decompensation in known cirrhosis)
- **Hepatomegaly** (palpable liver edge, may be hard/nodular)
- **Bruit** on auscultation (uncommon but pathognomonic)
- **Signs of chronic liver disease** (spider naevi, palmar erythema, gynaecomastia, testicular atrophy)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Metastatic liver cancer (e.g. from colorectal, breast)** – multiple lesions on imaging, history of primary cancer, CEUS shows hypo/hypervascular without washout
- **Cholangiocarcinoma** – hilar/peripheral mass, biliary duct dilation, elevated CA19-9, arterial enhancement with delayed washout (similar to HCC but different pattern)
- **Focal nodular hyperplasia (FNH)** – central scar, classic "spoke-wheel" on imaging, normal AFP
- **Hepatic adenoma** – in young women on OCP, subcapsular, hypervascular with risk of rupture, no cirrhosis
- **Haemangioma** – well-defined, hyperechoic on US, typical peripheral nodular enhancement on CT, no washout
- **Cirrhotic nodule / regenerative nodule** – no arterial hypervascularity, no washout; surveillance shows stability
- **Splitter:** **AFP >200 ng/mL + arterial hypervascularisation with delayed washout = HCC** (virtually diagnostic in cirrhosis). If AFP normal but imaging shows these features, still strongly consider HCC.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - **Ascitic tap** if new ascites (neutrophil count + culture; also check albumin and total protein to calculate SAAG)
  - **Urinalysis** – rule out UTI as precipitant of encephalopathy
  - **NEWS2 & GCS** – monitor for deterioration
  - **Fingerstick glucose** – hypoglycaemia in liver failure
- **Bloods:**
  - **FBC, U&E, LFTs, coagulation (INR), albumin**
  - **AFP** (elevated >200 ng/mL highly specific; normal does not exclude)
  - **Hepatitis B (HBsAg, anti-HBc), Hepatitis C (anti-HCV)**
  - **Blood cultures** if fever or signs of infection
  - **Ascitic fluid:** neutrophil count, culture, albumin, total protein
  - **New York Heart Association (NYHA) / MELD** – but not a blood test; calculate MELD using bilirubin, INR, creatinine
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** **Triphasic CT abdomen** (arterial, portal venous, delayed) – best for diagnosis and staging. Criteria: arterial hyperenhancement + delayed washout.
  - **Second-line:** **MRI liver with contrast** (if CT equivocal, or young patient to avoid radiation)
  - **Ultrasound with contrast (CEUS)** – useful for characterising lesions, but not for staging
  - **Chest CT** – for extrahepatic metastases
- **Special tests:**
  - **Liver biopsy** – only if imaging is atypical (e.g. AFP normal, imaging not diagnostic) and lesion is amenable; risk of bleeding in cirrhosis
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **LI-RADS (v2018)** – standardised reporting for liver nodules. **LR-5** (definitely HCC) requires arterial hyperenhancement + washout + capsule appearance or growth. **LR-4** (probably HCC) – consider biopsy.
  - **Non-invasive diagnosis (AASLD/EASL):** if cirrhotic + lesion >1cm + arterial hyperenhancement + delayed washout on one high-quality imaging = HCC. If >2cm + AFP >200 + arterial enhancement = may also be diagnostic.
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **Varices screening OGD** **BEFORE** starting any systemic therapy (especially bevacizumab) – risk of bleeding is high. If already on anti-angiogenic and new varices, stop and treat varices.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If bleeding: **resuscitate** (IV fluids, cross-match, blood transfusion, consider terlipressin if variceal bleed suspected) + **urgent OGD** (band ligation) + **antibiotics** (ceftriaxone 2g IV)
  - If encephalopathy: **lactulose** (15–30ml PO 6 hourly) + **rifaximin** 550mg BD if poor response; identify precipitant (bleed, infection, electrolyte disturbance)
  - If SBP: **IV antibiotics** (piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g q8h or ceftriaxone 2g IV) + **albumin** 1.5g/kg day1, 1g/kg day3 (if serum creatinine >1.5mg/dL or urea >30mmol/L)
  - If tumour rupture: **urgent interventional radiology** for embolisation; plus IV fluids, cross-match, analgesia
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **Confirm HCC diagnosis** with imaging (if not done) → **AFP, CT/MRI** – then **refer to liver MDT**
  - **Staging** (BCLC – Barcelona Clinic Liver Cancer): 0-A (early), B (intermediate), C (advanced), D (terminal)
  - **Child-Pugh score** (bilirubin, albumin, INR, ascites, encephalopathy) – guides treatment
  - **Treat underlying cirrhosis** – diuretics for ascites (spironolactone 50-200mg OD + furosemide 20-40mg OD), lactulose/rifaximin for encephalopathy, stop alcohol, check hepatitis B/C and treat if present
  - **VTE prophylaxis** – only after varices assessed (if no high-risk varices, use LMWH; if varices, use pneumatic compression)
  - **Nutrition** – oral nutrition with high protein, low sodium (if ascitic)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **MDT decision** – treatments depend on stage:
    - **BCLC 0-A:** surgical resection (if Child-Pugh A + no portal hypertension) or **ablation** (RFA/MWA) or **transplant** (within Milan criteria)
    - **BCLC B:** **TACE** (transarterial chemoembolisation)
    - **BCLC C:** **systemic therapy** – first-line **atezolizumab + bevacizumab** (if no variceal bleeding risk) or **lenvatinib** as monotherapy; second-line **sorafenib** (older), **regorafenib** (post-sorafenib)
    - **BCLC D:** **best supportive care** (symptom management, palliative care referral)
  - **Surveillance imaging** (CT/MRI) every 3-6 months for treated HCC to detect recurrence
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: **IV 0.9% saline** at 1-2mL/kg/h **if** hydrated, but fluid restrict (1-1.5L/day) in severe ascites/hyponatremia
  - VTE prophylaxis: **LMWH** (e.g., enoxaparin 20mg SC OD) **only if** variceal bleeding excluded (OGD done). Otherwise, **pneumatic compression stockings**
  - Diet: **normal protein, low sodium (<2g/day)** – encourage intake; nil by mouth if OGD planned
  - Observations: **Vitals 4-hourly** + **GCS** + **abdominal girth** daily (for ascites)
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Atezolizumab** (immunotherapy) **+ bevacizumab** (anti-VEGF): atezolizumab 1200mg IV over 60min q3w + bevacizumab 15mg/kg IV q3w until progression or unacceptable toxicity. *[Check local protocol for dosing schedules – ensure OGD for varices prior to bevacizumab]*
  - **Lenvatinib** 8mg PO OD (if <60kg) or 12mg OD (≥60kg) – for Child-Pugh A only
  - **Sorafenib** 400mg PO BD – for Child-Pugh A only; side effects: diarrhoea, hand-foot skin reaction
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Regorafenib** (160mg PO OD 3 weeks on/1 week off) – second-line after sorafenib
  - **TACE** – chemotherapeutic agent (e.g. doxorubicin 50mg/m²) + lipiodol + embolic particles – given by interventional radiologist
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Bevacizumab: **uncorrected variceal bleeding, active infection, recent surgery (within 28 days), uncontrolled hypertension**
  - Sorafenib/lenvatinib: **severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh B/C)** – not recommended; dose reduction if moderate
  - Atezolizumab: caution in **autoimmune disease** (may flare), **immune-related adverse events** (pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitis, thyroiditis)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Sorafenib: no adjustment for mild–moderate renal; avoid if CrCl <30mL/min. Child-Pugh B: consider dose reduction to 200mg BD (expert opinion only)
  - Lenvatinib: adjust for severe renal impairment (CrCl <30mL/min) – dose reduction per SmPC
  - No dose adjustment for atezolizumab/bevacizumab in mild–moderate renal/hepatic impairment; no data in severe
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Bevacizumab – not screening for varices** before first dose can cause fatal variceal haemorrhage. Ensure OGD within 6 months before starting.
  - **Sorafenib/lenvatinib – dose escalation** without monitoring for diarrhoea, electrolyte disturbances, skin toxicity. Start at full dose in Child-Pugh A only; do not double dose if missed.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - **Variceal haemorrhage** (especially if untreated varices)
  - **Tumour rupture** – haemoperitoneum, hypovolaemic shock
  - **SBP / sepsis**
  - **Hepatic encephalopathy** (precipitated by infection, bleeding, electrolyte imbalance, medications)
  - **Renal failure** (hepatorenal syndrome)
  - **Immune-related adverse events** (if on checkpoint inhibitors; e.g., colitis, pneumonitis, hepatitis, thyroiditis)
- **Chronic:**
  - **Portal hypertension** → ascites, splenomegaly, varices, encephalopathy
  - **Progression of cirrhosis** → decompensation, listed for transplant
  - **Metastatic spread** (lung, bone, lymph nodes)
  - **Cachexia / malnutrition**
  - **Treatment side effects** – TACE: post-embolisation syndrome (fever, pain, nausea); systemic therapy: hand-foot skin reaction, fatigue, diarrhoea
sections.Prognosis: - **Highly dependent on stage at diagnosis and liver function.** For **BCLC 0-A** (single <2cm, Child-Pugh A): 5-year survival >70% with curative treatment. **BCLC C** (advanced): median survival 6–10 months with systemic therapy. **BCLC D** (terminal): weeks to months. Tumour size, number, vascular invasion, AFP >400, and Child-Pugh/MELD score are key predictors.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
1. **Always perform an OGD** before starting bevacizumab (or any anti-angiogenic) – document varices and band if needed. If high-risk varices, band then re-scope before starting.
2. **Calculate Child-Pugh and BCLC stage on every patient** – this drives treatment decisions. Do not start sorafenib/lenvatinib if Child-Pugh B/C without expert discussion.
3. **In a known cirrhotic with new HCC, treat the underlying cirrhosis first** (initiate diuretics for ascites, lactulose for encephalopathy) – optimise liver function before any cancer therapy.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
1. **Starting sorafenib/lenvatinib without assessing varices** – can cause fatal variceal bleed. Always screen before first dose.
2. **Ignoring mild encephalopathy or ascites as "just the cancer"** – treat decompensation aggressively; it may be reversible and change stage from BCLC C to B (if decompensated, treat as D).
3. **Using NSAIDs for pain** – risk of acute kidney injury, variceal bleeding, and gastric ulceration. Prefer paracetamol (1g QDS max) or weak opioids (codeine/tramadol with caution).
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - **Worsening jaundice** (yellow skin/eyes) or **dark urine**
  - **New abdominal distension** (ascites) or **breathlessness**
  - **Any bleeding** (haematemesis, melaena, haematochezia, nosebleeds)
  - **Fever** >38°C – may indicate SBP or cholangitis
  - **Confusion or personality change** – signs of encephalopathy
- **Follow-up:**
  - **Outpatient hepatology** within 2 weeks (or sooner if on systemic therapy) – for MDT discussion and treatment plan
  - **Surveillance imaging** – 3-monthly CT/MRI for treated HCC; 6-monthly US for high-risk cirrhosis
  - **Varice surveillance** – OGD every 1-3 years (if no varices) or 1-yearly (if small varices)
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/ICU if:**
  - **NEWS2 ≥5** or sustained tachycardia (>110) / hypotension (SBP <90)
  - **Acute variceal haemorrhage** – call on-call gastroenterology + blood bank + ICU
  - **GCS <13** or **new confusion** – urgent CT head to exclude bleed, call hepatology
  - **Lactate >4 mmol/L** without clear cause
  - **Oliguria** (<0.5mL/kg/h) with rising creatinine – risk of hepatorenal syndrome; contact nephrology
  - **Severe sepsis** (on IV antibiotics, not improving) – consider ICU for vasopressors
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - **Encephalopathy grade 3 or 4** (obtunded) – ICU for airway protection
  - **INR >2.5** or **platelets <50** – risk of spontaneous bleeding; discuss with haematology / hepatology
  - **Bilirubin >200µmol/L** in a patient on systemic therapy – stop treatment and discuss with consultant
  - **For tumour rupture:** **immediate call to interventional radiology / on-call surgeon** – not stable for ward management

rawMarkdown:
## Hepatocellular Carcinoma
**One-liner:** Primary liver malignancy arising in chronic liver disease, often cirrhosis. Leading cause of death in cirrhosis, diagnosed on surveillance or when decompensation occurs.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Variceal haemorrhage** – haematemesis, melaena with tachycardia/hypotension. Single finding: **fresh blood on nasogastric tube** (if placed) or **visible red/blue varices on oesophagogastroduodenoscopy (OGD) – but do not delay resuscitation for endoscopy.**
- **Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP)** – fever, abdominal pain, encephalopathy. Single finding: **ascitic neutrophil count >250 cells/µL** (but start antibiotics if clinical suspicion + high lactate).
- **Hepatic encephalopathy** – confusion, asterixis, drowsy. Single finding: **reversed sleep-wake cycle + asterixis** (not just sepsis).
- **Tumour rupture with haemorrhage** – sudden severe right upper quadrant pain, hypotension. Single finding: **free fluid on bedside US + hypotension** (rare but immediately lethal; needs embolisation or emergency surgery).

### Presentation
- Often **asymptomatic** – discovered on surveillance US in cirrhotic patients
- **Right upper quadrant pain** (dull ache or sharp if rupture)
- **Unintentional weight loss / anorexia**
- **Worsening jaundice, ascites, or encephalopathy** (new decompensation in known cirrhosis)
- **Hepatomegaly** (palpable liver edge, may be hard/nodular)
- **Bruit** on auscultation (uncommon but pathognomonic)
- **Signs of chronic liver disease** (spider naevi, palmar erythema, gynaecomastia, testicular atrophy)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Metastatic liver cancer (e.g. from colorectal, breast)** – multiple lesions on imaging, history of primary cancer, CEUS shows hypo/hypervascular without washout
- **Cholangiocarcinoma** – hilar/peripheral mass, biliary duct dilation, elevated CA19-9, arterial enhancement with delayed washout (similar to HCC but different pattern)
- **Focal nodular hyperplasia (FNH)** – central scar, classic "spoke-wheel" on imaging, normal AFP
- **Hepatic adenoma** – in young women on OCP, subcapsular, hypervascular with risk of rupture, no cirrhosis
- **Haemangioma** – well-defined, hyperechoic on US, typical peripheral nodular enhancement on CT, no washout
- **Cirrhotic nodule / regenerative nodule** – no arterial hypervascularity, no washout; surveillance shows stability
- **Splitter:** **AFP >200 ng/mL + arterial hypervascularisation with delayed washout = HCC** (virtually diagnostic in cirrhosis). If AFP normal but imaging shows these features, still strongly consider HCC.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - **Ascitic tap** if new ascites (neutrophil count + culture; also check albumin and total protein to calculate SAAG)
  - **Urinalysis** – rule out UTI as precipitant of encephalopathy
  - **NEWS2 & GCS** – monitor for deterioration
  - **Fingerstick glucose** – hypoglycaemia in liver failure
- **Bloods:**
  - **FBC, U&E, LFTs, coagulation (INR), albumin**
  - **AFP** (elevated >200 ng/mL highly specific; normal does not exclude)
  - **Hepatitis B (HBsAg, anti-HBc), Hepatitis C (anti-HCV)**
  - **Blood cultures** if fever or signs of infection
  - **Ascitic fluid:** neutrophil count, culture, albumin, total protein
  - **New York Heart Association (NYHA) / MELD** – but not a blood test; calculate MELD using bilirubin, INR, creatinine
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** **Triphasic CT abdomen** (arterial, portal venous, delayed) – best for diagnosis and staging. Criteria: arterial hyperenhancement + delayed washout.
  - **Second-line:** **MRI liver with contrast** (if CT equivocal, or young patient to avoid radiation)
  - **Ultrasound with contrast (CEUS)** – useful for characterising lesions, but not for staging
  - **Chest CT** – for extrahepatic metastases
- **Special tests:**
  - **Liver biopsy** – only if imaging is atypical (e.g. AFP normal, imaging not diagnostic) and lesion is amenable; risk of bleeding in cirrhosis
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **LI-RADS (v2018)** – standardised reporting for liver nodules. **LR-5** (definitely HCC) requires arterial hyperenhancement + washout + capsule appearance or growth. **LR-4** (probably HCC) – consider biopsy.
  - **Non-invasive diagnosis (AASLD/EASL):** if cirrhotic + lesion >1cm + arterial hyperenhancement + delayed washout on one high-quality imaging = HCC. If >2cm + AFP >200 + arterial enhancement = may also be diagnostic.
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **Varices screening OGD** **BEFORE** starting any systemic therapy (especially bevacizumab) – risk of bleeding is high. If already on anti-angiogenic and new varices, stop and treat varices.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If bleeding: **resuscitate** (IV fluids, cross-match, blood transfusion, consider terlipressin if variceal bleed suspected) + **urgent OGD** (band ligation) + **antibiotics** (ceftriaxone 2g IV)
  - If encephalopathy: **lactulose** (15–30ml PO 6 hourly) + **rifaximin** 550mg BD if poor response; identify precipitant (bleed, infection, electrolyte disturbance)
  - If SBP: **IV antibiotics** (piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g q8h or ceftriaxone 2g IV) + **albumin** 1.5g/kg day1, 1g/kg day3 (if serum creatinine >1.5mg/dL or urea >30mmol/L)
  - If tumour rupture: **urgent interventional radiology** for embolisation; plus IV fluids, cross-match, analgesia
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - **Confirm HCC diagnosis** with imaging (if not done) → **AFP, CT/MRI** – then **refer to liver MDT**
  - **Staging** (BCLC – Barcelona Clinic Liver Cancer): 0-A (early), B (intermediate), C (advanced), D (terminal)
  - **Child-Pugh score** (bilirubin, albumin, INR, ascites, encephalopathy) – guides treatment
  - **Treat underlying cirrhosis** – diuretics for ascites (spironolactone 50-200mg OD + furosemide 20-40mg OD), lactulose/rifaximin for encephalopathy, stop alcohol, check hepatitis B/C and treat if present
  - **VTE prophylaxis** – only after varices assessed (if no high-risk varices, use LMWH; if varices, use pneumatic compression)
  - **Nutrition** – oral nutrition with high protein, low sodium (if ascitic)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **MDT decision** – treatments depend on stage:
    - **BCLC 0-A:** surgical resection (if Child-Pugh A + no portal hypertension) or **ablation** (RFA/MWA) or **transplant** (within Milan criteria)
    - **BCLC B:** **TACE** (transarterial chemoembolisation)
    - **BCLC C:** **systemic therapy** – first-line **atezolizumab + bevacizumab** (if no variceal bleeding risk) or **lenvatinib** as monotherapy; second-line **sorafenib** (older), **regorafenib** (post-sorafenib)
    - **BCLC D:** **best supportive care** (symptom management, palliative care referral)
  - **Surveillance imaging** (CT/MRI) every 3-6 months for treated HCC to detect recurrence
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: **IV 0.9% saline** at 1-2mL/kg/h **if** hydrated, but fluid restrict (1-1.5L/day) in severe ascites/hyponatremia
  - VTE prophylaxis: **LMWH** (e.g., enoxaparin 20mg SC OD) **only if** variceal bleeding excluded (OGD done). Otherwise, **pneumatic compression stockings**
  - Diet: **normal protein, low sodium (<2g/day)** – encourage intake; nil by mouth if OGD planned
  - Observations: **Vitals 4-hourly** + **GCS** + **abdominal girth** daily (for ascites)

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Atezolizumab** (immunotherapy) **+ bevacizumab** (anti-VEGF): atezolizumab 1200mg IV over 60min q3w + bevacizumab 15mg/kg IV q3w until progression or unacceptable toxicity. *[Check local protocol for dosing schedules – ensure OGD for varices prior to bevacizumab]*
  - **Lenvatinib** 8mg PO OD (if <60kg) or 12mg OD (≥60kg) – for Child-Pugh A only
  - **Sorafenib** 400mg PO BD – for Child-Pugh A only; side effects: diarrhoea, hand-foot skin reaction
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Regorafenib** (160mg PO OD 3 weeks on/1 week off) – second-line after sorafenib
  - **TACE** – chemotherapeutic agent (e.g. doxorubicin 50mg/m²) + lipiodol + embolic particles – given by interventional radiologist
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Bevacizumab: **uncorrected variceal bleeding, active infection, recent surgery (within 28 days), uncontrolled hypertension**
  - Sorafenib/lenvatinib: **severe hepatic impairment (Child-Pugh B/C)** – not recommended; dose reduction if moderate
  - Atezolizumab: caution in **autoimmune disease** (may flare), **immune-related adverse events** (pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitis, thyroiditis)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Sorafenib: no adjustment for mild–moderate renal; avoid if CrCl <30mL/min. Child-Pugh B: consider dose reduction to 200mg BD (expert opinion only)
  - Lenvatinib: adjust for severe renal impairment (CrCl <30mL/min) – dose reduction per SmPC
  - No dose adjustment for atezolizumab/bevacizumab in mild–moderate renal/hepatic impairment; no data in severe
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Bevacizumab – not screening for varices** before first dose can cause fatal variceal haemorrhage. Ensure OGD within 6 months before starting.
  - **Sorafenib/lenvatinib – dose escalation** without monitoring for diarrhoea, electrolyte disturbances, skin toxicity. Start at full dose in Child-Pugh A only; do not double dose if missed.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - **Variceal haemorrhage** (especially if untreated varices)
  - **Tumour rupture** – haemoperitoneum, hypovolaemic shock
  - **SBP / sepsis**
  - **Hepatic encephalopathy** (precipitated by infection, bleeding, electrolyte imbalance, medications)
  - **Renal failure** (hepatorenal syndrome)
  - **Immune-related adverse events** (if on checkpoint inhibitors; e.g., colitis, pneumonitis, hepatitis, thyroiditis)
- **Chronic:**
  - **Portal hypertension** → ascites, splenomegaly, varices, encephalopathy
  - **Progression of cirrhosis** → decompensation, listed for transplant
  - **Metastatic spread** (lung, bone, lymph nodes)
  - **Cachexia / malnutrition**
  - **Treatment side effects** – TACE: post-embolisation syndrome (fever, pain, nausea); systemic therapy: hand-foot skin reaction, fatigue, diarrhoea

### Prognosis
- **Highly dependent on stage at diagnosis and liver function.** For **BCLC 0-A** (single <2cm, Child-Pugh A): 5-year survival >70% with curative treatment. **BCLC C** (advanced): median survival 6–10 months with systemic therapy. **BCLC D** (terminal): weeks to months. Tumour size, number, vascular invasion, AFP >400, and Child-Pugh/MELD score are key predictors.

### Pearls (positive actions)
1. **Always perform an OGD** before starting bevacizumab (or any anti-angiogenic) – document varices and band if needed. If high-risk varices, band then re-scope before starting.
2. **Calculate Child-Pugh and BCLC stage on every patient** – this drives treatment decisions. Do not start sorafenib/lenvatinib if Child-Pugh B/C without expert discussion.
3. **In a known cirrhotic with new HCC, treat the underlying cirrhosis first** (initiate diuretics for ascites, lactulose for encephalopathy) – optimise liver function before any cancer therapy.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
1. **Starting sorafenib/lenvatinib without assessing varices** – can cause fatal variceal bleed. Always screen before first dose.
2. **Ignoring mild encephalopathy or ascites as "just the cancer"** – treat decompensation aggressively; it may be reversible and change stage from BCLC C to B (if decompensated, treat as D).
3. **Using NSAIDs for pain** – risk of acute kidney injury, variceal bleeding, and gastric ulceration. Prefer paracetamol (1g QDS max) or weak opioids (codeine/tramadol with caution).

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - **Worsening jaundice** (yellow skin/eyes) or **dark urine**
  - **New abdominal distension** (ascites) or **breathlessness**
  - **Any bleeding** (haematemesis, melaena, haematochezia, nosebleeds)
  - **Fever** >38°C – may indicate SBP or cholangitis
  - **Confusion or personality change** – signs of encephalopathy
- **Follow-up:**
  - **Outpatient hepatology** within 2 weeks (or sooner if on systemic therapy) – for MDT discussion and treatment plan
  - **Surveillance imaging** – 3-monthly CT/MRI for treated HCC; 6-monthly US for high-risk cirrhosis
  - **Varice surveillance** – OGD every 1-3 years (if no varices) or 1-yearly (if small varices)

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/ICU if:**
  - **NEWS2 ≥5** or sustained tachycardia (>110) / hypotension (SBP <90)
  - **Acute variceal haemorrhage** – call on-call gastroenterology + blood bank + ICU
  - **GCS <13** or **new confusion** – urgent CT head to exclude bleed, call hepatology
  - **Lactate >4 mmol/L** without clear cause
  - **Oliguria** (<0.5mL/kg/h) with rising creatinine – risk of hepatorenal syndrome; contact nephrology
  - **Severe sepsis** (on IV antibiotics, not improving) – consider ICU for vasopressors
- **Specific thresholds:**
  - **Encephalopathy grade 3 or 4** (obtunded) – ICU for airway protection
  - **INR >2.5** or **platelets <50** – risk of spontaneous bleeding; discuss with haematology / hepatology
  - **Bilirubin >200µmol/L** in a patient on systemic therapy – stop treatment and discuss with consultant
  - **For tumour rupture:** **immediate call to interventional radiology / on-call surgeon** – not stable for ward management
